_meta.role: Glossary of the Sufi technical terms used in the Chessboard. Each term carries its Arabic script and its scientific (diacritical) transliteration.
_meta.source: Termes tirés du commentaire d'al-Hāshimī (Michon, ARChè 1998) et de l'usage soufi classique.

termes.def: The soul, and more precisely the carnal soul that drives toward evil (al-nafs al-ammāra) until it is purified.
The heart: the subtle organ of knowledge and the receptacle of the divine Presence, distinct from the bodily heart.
The spirit: the first creature, the closest to God, belonging to the “world of the command” (ʿālam al-amr).
Trusting surrender: entrusting one's affairs to God after employing the means, without leaning on them.
Extinction: the fading of the awareness of the separate self, which effaces itself before the sole Reality of God.
Permanence (or subsistence) in God: what remains after extinction, when the servant subsists through God and not through himself.
“Taste”: knowledge through direct, lived experience, as opposed to theoretical learning.
Spiritual poverty: the awareness of having, of oneself, neither being nor power — all comes from God.
Direct knowledge of God (gnosis), received from Him, distinct from knowledge acquired by study.
The essential Reality: the inner, true meaning, beyond the outward form of the Law.
The revealed Law: the outward prescriptions, which are “the words of the Prophet”.
The way: the inner method of the journey, which “is none other than the acts of the Prophet”.
The divine Presence: the station where the servant stands under the gaze and in the nearness of God.
Disguised gradual fall: an apparent favour by which the soul is imperceptibly drawn downward — “God is the best of strategists”.
Perfect servanthood: the state of fully consented worship, “the noblest and highest of stations”.
Lordship: the quality of Master proper to God alone, of which the servant must claim nothing.
The lordly voice: a subtle inner call, perceived by the heart, speaking through the tongue of the Spirit.
Ardent desire. Figurative (majāzī) when it bears on creatures, real (ḥaqīqī) when it bears on the Creator.
Love, especially love for the Creator — distinguished from ḥubb, love for creatures.
Contraction: the tightening of the heart, a state of withdrawal and inner trial.
Expansion: the dilation and joy of the heart, the opposite of contraction (qabḍ).
Spiritual intoxication: a rapture in which ordinary lucidity is veiled.
Perplexity: the bewilderment of one who seeks God without yet knowing how to reach Him.
Reverential awe: to fear God out of regard for His majesty, and to hasten toward Him.
Fear: dread of punishment, which turns into an impulse toward God (“fear them not, but fear Me”).
Contentment: to fully accept God's decrees, in ease as in trial.
Absolute sincerity: to act for God alone, without regard for others or for oneself.
Affectation, ostentation: to act so as to be seen and praised by people.
Right inner and outer bearing: the good manners of the heart toward God and creatures.
The spiritual guide who leads the traveller and protects him from the snares of the road.
The master: a living, sincere guide who has himself walked the way under a direction.
Sainthood: the state of the one “near” to God (walī), surrounded by His protection.
Prophethood: the degree where God imparts sciences and secrets, and teaches Wisdom.
The Kingdom: the sensible, manifest world; the first of the three degrees (mulk, malakūt, jabarūt).
The Dominion: the subtle world of spirits and angels; corresponds to faith (īmān).
The Immensity: the Presence of the mystery, source of all things; corresponds to excellence (iḥsān).
The human world: the gross manifestation, the term of the descending return toward the Kingdom.
The divine world: the Presence that embraces the essence, the names and the qualities.
Attesting Unity: to recognise that there is no acting reality but God.
The mystery, the unseen: that which escapes the senses and is known only to God.
Certainty. Three degrees: the science (ʿilm), the eye (ʿayn) and the truth (ḥaqq) of certainty.
The station: a stable, acquired and lasting degree of the journey.
The state: a passing grace that descends upon the heart, as opposed to the stable station (maqām).
The traveller: the one who actively journeys on the way toward God.
Constrained: the passage through the squares of the Chessboard is “forced” (ijbārī) more than freely chosen.
The whispering: the insidious suggestion of the tempter or of the soul.
The isthmus: the intermediate world between the sensible and the spiritual, between death and resurrection.
The here-below: the present world and its lures, opposed to the life to come (al-ākhira).
The world of the command: the plane from which the spirit and the realities proceed, by the sole “Be!”.
